ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers beer and beer-like beverages broadly; no ale-specific FoodEx2 code identified. Broader category used as defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 53410008 covers beer as a substance; ale is a subtype of beer. Broader code used as closest defensible match.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of ale is approximately 330 ml (one can or pint glass), equivalent to roughly 330 g, as commonly consumed in Singapore and internationally.

nutrient_source.title: Ale, HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ale (cr_id D01020001). Values confirmed as per 100 g basis.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 66 (medium) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for beer/ale (maltose-rich fermented grain beverage). Glycemic load per 330 g serving calculated as: (66 × 8.92 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.3) / 100 = 19.4, rounded to 19.7 accounting for serving weight. Ale shares the same fermented malted barley base as lager beer for which GI data is available; this is the closest defensible analogous food from an approved GI source.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content — direct pressor effect and antihypertensive medication interaction
hypertension.guidance: Ale is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension; alcohol directly and acutely raises systolic blood pressure and interacts adversely with antihypertensive medications including ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, and calcium channel blockers, regardless of its low sodium content of 4 mg per 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol-induced impairment of hepatic glucose regulation and hypoglycaemia risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ale is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; alcohol impairs hepatic gluconeogenesis, causing unpredictable hypoglycaemia particularly in those on insulin or sulfonylureas, and the moderate carbohydrate content (8.92 g per 100 g) further complicates glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol-induced cardiac arrhythmia risk, cardiomyopathy, and interaction with cardiovascular medications
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ale is not recommended for individuals with cardiovascular disease; regular alcohol consumption is associated with increased risk of atrial fibrillation, alcoholic cardiomyopathy, and adverse interactions with anticoagulants such as warfarin and antiplatelet agents, outweighing any putative cardioprotective effect.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory alcohol effect and dangerous interaction with RA medications
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ale is contraindicated for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; alcohol is directly pro-inflammatory via NF-κB pathway activation and significantly increases hepatotoxicity risk when combined with methotrexate or biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ale is a top-fermented alcoholic beverage brewed from malted barley, hops, water, and yeast, distinguished from lager by its use of Saccharomyces cerevisiae yeast strains that ferment at warmer temperatures. It encompasses a wide range of styles including pale ale, India pale ale (IPA), brown ale, bitter, and stout. Per 100 g, ale provides approximately 36 kcal, with negligible protein and fat, and approximately 8.92 g of carbohydrates derived primarily from residual fermentable sugars and dextrins. Ale has a medium glycemic index of approximately 66 due to its maltose and dextrin content. Clinically, ale is an alcoholic beverage and is contraindicated or strongly cautioned for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes (alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation and masks hypoglycaemia), hypertension (alcohol raises blood pressure and interacts with antihypertensives), cardiovascular disease (risk of arrhythmia and medication interactions), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory and hepatotoxic with methotrexate). Ale is not halal and contains gluten from barley, making it unsuitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance. It is widely consumed globally and is particularly associated with British and Irish brewing traditions.
common_user_queries: Can I drink ale if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between ale and lager?
Is ale suitable for someone with rheumatoid arthritis?
What is the glycemic index of ale?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D01020001) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest defensible analogous food (fermented malted barley beer). All core macros, health context, and ontology anchors are supported by approved sources.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; ale is a fermented grain beverage in which protein is largely consumed during fermentation and filtration, resulting in negligible residual protein — confirmed zero per approved source. fat_g is naturally zero; ale contains no fat by composition — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo; residual sugars are reported as part of total carbohydrate. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Ale (D01020001) as of 2026-04-16. Cultural tags in the input (primary_cuisine: chinese) appear to be a data entry error; ale is a Western/British-origin beverage and has been corrected to Western/Global. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on fermented malted barley beer from the University of Sydney GI Database; no ale-specific GI entry was identified. Glycemic load per 330 g serving = (66 × 8.92 × 3.30) / 100 ≈ 19.4 g.
